event: Stone Age hunter-gatherers, ancestors of the Twa, inhabit the forests and highlands of the region that would become Rwanda.
Bantu-speaking agriculturalists begin migrating into the Great Lakes region, introducing crop cultivation and iron-working.
Iron Age communities establish farming settlements and clear forests across the Rwandan highlands.
Pastoralist and agricultural societies develop across the hills, gradually forming the clan-based social structure of the region.
Cattle-herding and farming communities coalesce into small chiefdoms and lineage groups in the central highlands.
The Kingdom of Rwanda emerges around the Nyiginya clan on the central plateau under early Mwami (kings).
King Ruganzu II Ndori consolidates and expands the Nyiginya kingdom, strengthening centralized royal authority.
The Rwandan kingdom develops the ubuhake clientship system binding patrons and clients through cattle exchange.
King Yuhi IV Gahindiro reigns, further centralizing administration through chiefs of land, cattle, and military.
King Kigeli IV Rwabugiri accedes to the throne and undertakes major military expansion and administrative reforms.
The Berlin Conference of the Scramble for Africa assigns Rwanda to the German sphere of influence in East Africa.
German explorer Gustav Adolf von Götzen becomes the first European to enter Rwanda, reaching the royal court.
Germany establishes colonial rule, incorporating Rwanda into German East Africa through indirect rule via the monarchy.
Catholic White Fathers missionaries arrive and establish the first mission stations in Rwanda.
Belgian forces occupy Rwanda during World War I, ousting the German colonial administration.
The League of Nations grants Belgium a mandate over Ruanda-Urundi, formalizing Belgian colonial control.
Belgian authorities introduce ethnic identity cards classifying people rigidly as Hutu, Tutsi, or Twa.
Ruanda-Urundi becomes a United Nations Trust Territory administered by Belgium after World War II.
The Hutu Revolution erupts, with widespread violence against Tutsi and the beginning of mass displacement.
A referendum abolishes the Tutsi monarchy and Rwanda is declared a republic with Grégoire Kayibanda as leader.
Rwanda gains full independence from Belgium on 1 July and joins the United Nations as a sovereign state.
Exiled Tutsi rebels invade from Burundi, triggering reprisal massacres of Tutsi within Rwanda.
Defence minister Juvénal Habyarimana seizes power in a military coup, ousting President Kayibanda.
Habyarimana establishes the MRND as the sole legal party, creating a one-party state.
The Rwandan Patriotic Front (RPF), composed largely of Tutsi exiles, invades from Uganda, starting the Rwandan Civil War.
The Arusha Accords are signed between the government and the RPF to establish a power-sharing transition.
After President Habyarimana's plane is shot down, the genocide against the Tutsi kills an estimated 800,000 people in about 100 days before the RPF takes power.
The UN International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda is established in Arusha to prosecute genocide perpetrators.
Rwanda invades Zaire to pursue génocidaires, helping ignite the First Congo War.
Rwanda backs rebels in the Second Congo War, drawing the country into a wider regional conflict.
Paul Kagame becomes President of Rwanda after the resignation of Pasteur Bizimungu.
Community-based Gacaca courts are launched nationwide to try genocide cases and promote reconciliation.
A new constitution is adopted and Kagame wins the first post-genocide presidential election.
Rwanda joins the East African Community, deepening regional economic integration.
Rwanda switches its official language of instruction from French to English and bans plastic bags nationwide.
Rwanda is admitted to the Commonwealth of Nations despite having no British colonial past.
The Gacaca court system formally closes after trying nearly two million genocide-related cases.
A referendum approves constitutional amendments allowing President Kagame to seek additional terms in office.
Paul Kagame is re-elected president with nearly 99 percent of the vote.
Rwanda hosts the signing of the African Continental Free Trade Area agreement in Kigali.
Rwanda deploys troops to Mozambique's Cabo Delgado to combat an Islamist insurgency.
Rwanda commemorates the 30th anniversary of the genocide as Kagame wins re-election with over 99 percent of the vote.
